# Extração: 05.01-CA Emo Story Training

- **Fonte:** 05.01-CA Emo Story Training.docx
- **Tipo:** DOCX
- **Tamanho:** 10.2 KB

---

The following is a GENERAL structure of how emo copy looks. 



It’s not a hard and fast rule. 



Sometimes it’s just an emo lead but nothing else…



Other times the whole VSL is centered around an emo story. 



But it’s a rule of thumb…





1. Tease emotional horror story right out the gate (lead).

2. Say how this awful thing led to a positive breakthrough that you’re going to share with them inside this letter/video. 

3. Then rest of lead.

4. After the lead, back to story. “So like I said, my name is Bob and I’m just like you. I thought I was healthy and things were fine. So when I first started showing symptoms, I didn’t think much about it. But then suddenly, SHOCKING THING happened (which is a callback to what was teased in the lead).

5. Then share the story of the SHOCKING THING. Give them some details, make it real and believable, but don’t go forever. Instead it should be like 500-1000 words.

6. And this SHOCKING THING is what finally broke the camel’s back. I knew something had to change, so I set out on a journey to change it. 

7. But there were setbacks. Traditional advice didn’t work. Traditional solutions didn’t work. Obstacles. I wanted to give up, but then I reached a turning point.

8. At the turning point, either they meet their sensei/Sherpa, or they figure it out on their own in some peculiar way.

9. This is when the UM of the Problem and Solution occur. So Avatar realizes the REAL cause of their problem isn’t “X” which everyone thinks, but “Y” which nobody thinks. Here’s proof (studies, quotes, etc). 

10. Now that avatar knows REAL cause of problem, changes his thinking about the solution. Maybe this is simpler than he thought. He comes up with a hypothesis, or he asks an expert who gives him a hypothesis, to be explored and validated.

11. Upon testing his hypothesis, he gets some immediate validation. Looks at research/studies/facts, and it seems to make sense. Actually, finds a lot of evidence that this is the case. 

12. But realizes just because research supports an idea, doesn’t mean it will work in “real world” settings.

13. So he has to make himself a guinea pig, or find an expert who has already tested these ideas in the real world “ie. Clinical trials” to see for himself.

14. Ideally finds that the research IS accurate, however there are a few more tweaks that can make it even better. But the point is that it works!

15. But just because it worked for avatar, will it work for others? Avatar shares solution with others and lo-and-behold, it works for them too!

16. So at this point, Avatar knows he needs to share this with the rest of the world. So he start’s getting it packaged together. There are setbacks. It’s frustrating and he wants to give up or quit but keeps pushing forward. And ultimately, PRODUCT is born. 





Examples:
